source.title: IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Bacterial Rhinosinusitis in Children and Adults

signals: diagnosis and initial management of suspected acute bacterial rhinosinusitis
management based on risk assessment for antimicrobial resistance

extraction.summary: Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis (ABRS) is inflammation of nasal and paranasal sinus mucosa lasting up to 4 weeks, representing 2-10% of acute rhinosinusitis cases with most being viral. Guidelines address diagnostic criteria to distinguish bacterial from viral causes and appropriate empiric antibiotic selection to reduce overuse and antimicrobial resistance.

population.age_criteria: Children and adults - specific considerations for <2 years, daycare attendance, >65 years as high-risk factors
population.risk_criteria: High-risk factors include daycare attendance, age <2 or >65 years, recent hospitalization, antibiotic use within past month, immunocompromised status, severe infection with systemic toxicity
population.disease_definition: Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis defined as bacterial inflammation of nasal and paranasal sinus mucosa lasting up to 4 weeks, distinguished from viral causes by specific clinical criteria
population.exclusions: Viral upper respiratory infection, non-infectious causes, structural abnormalities
population.severity_staging: Severe infection defined as evidence of systemic toxicity with fever ≥39°C (102°F) and threat of suppurative complications

key_thresholds.context: Persistent symptoms without improvement
Severe onset with purulent discharge and facial pain
At beginning of illness for severe presentation
Double-sickening pattern after initial improvement
Geographic regions requiring high-dose therapy
Persistent symptoms lasting >10 days without improvement
High fever for severe symptoms presentation
At least 3-4 consecutive days at illness onset
Gold standard diagnostic threshold from sinus aspiration
Persistent symptoms without clinical improvement
Severe symptoms with purulent discharge or facial pain for 3-4 days
Associated with positive sinus culture (OR 4.63)
Double-sickening pattern after typical viral URI
Indicator for high-dose therapy and severe infection
Threshold for considering high-dose amoxicillin-clavulanate
US surveillance data 2005-2007
High resistance threshold contraindicating empiric macrolide use
High resistance threshold contraindicating empiric TMP/SMX use
High resistance threshold contraindicating empiric TMP/SMX use
Recovery rate from sinus cultures in ABRS patients
MRSA recovery rate from sinus cultures (2000-2006)
Incidence in children at 2-12 months follow-up
Expected clinical improvement timeframe
Time to consider alternative therapy if worsening
96% of patients have negative cultures by day 3
Short-course antibiotic therapy
Standard antibiotic course duration

recommendations.recommendation: Use specific clinical presentations to identify ABRS: persistent symptoms ≥10 days, severe onset with high fever and purulent discharge for 3-4 days, or worsening after initial URI improvement
recommendations.notes: Three distinct clinical presentations for bacterial vs viral differentiation

recommendations.recommendation: Initiate empiric antimicrobial therapy immediately upon clinical diagnosis of ABRS
recommendations.notes: Based on clinical criteria in recommendation 1

recommendations.recommendation: Use amoxicillin-clavulanate rather than amoxicillin alone for empiric therapy in children
recommendations.notes: First-line therapy for pediatric ABRS

recommendations.recommendation: Use high-dose amoxicillin-clavulanate for high-risk patients or high-resistance areas
recommendations.notes: 2g BID adults or 90mg/kg/day BID children for high-risk scenarios

recommendations.recommendation: Use clinical criteria rather than imaging studies for diagnosis of uncomplicated ABRS
recommendations.notes: Normal imaging can rule out ABRS, but abnormal imaging cannot confirm bacterial infection

recommendations.recommendation: Improve appropriate use of first-line antibiotics for presumptive ABRS diagnosis
recommendations.notes: Primary goal to reduce excessive antimicrobial use in viral infections

recommendations.recommendation: Consider amoxicillin vs amoxicillin-clavulanate for initial empiric therapy
recommendations.notes: Selection based on evolving antibiotic susceptibility profiles

recommendations.recommendation: Use 3 clinical presentations to identify ABRS: persistent symptoms ≥10 days, severe symptoms with high fever ≥39°C and purulent discharge for 3-4 days, or worsening after 5-6 days of initial improvement
recommendations.notes: Improves differentiation between bacterial and viral rhinosinusitis

recommendations.recommendation: Initiate empiric antimicrobial therapy as soon as clinical diagnosis of ABRS is established
recommendations.notes: Contrasts with watchful waiting approach

recommendations.recommendation: Amoxicillin-clavulanate rather than amoxicillin alone for empiric therapy in children
recommendations.notes: Due to increasing prevalence of β-lactamase producing H. influenzae

recommendations.recommendation: High-dose amoxicillin-clavulanate for high-risk patients
recommendations.notes: For regions with high PNS S. pneumoniae rates or severe infections

recommendations.recommendation: β-lactam agent rather than respiratory fluoroquinolone for initial therapy
recommendations.notes: No superior efficacy demonstrated with fluoroquinolones

recommendations.recommendation: Macrolides (clarithromycin and azithromycin) are not recommended for empiric therapy due to high rates of resistance among S. pneumoniae (≥30%)
recommendations.notes: High-level resistance mediated by ermB cannot be overcome during therapy

recommendations.recommendation: TMP/SMX is not recommended for empiric therapy due to high rates of resistance among both S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae (≥30%-40%)
recommendations.notes: Associated with rare but severe adverse reactions including toxic epidermal necrolysis

recommendations.recommendation: Doxycycline may be used as an alternative regimen to amoxicillin-clavulanate for initial empiric antimicrobial therapy of ABRS in adults
recommendations.notes: Remains highly active against respiratory pathogens with excellent PK/PD properties

recommendations.recommendation: Second- and third-generation oral cephalosporins are no longer recommended for empiric monotherapy of ABRS owing to variable rates of resistance among S. pneumoniae
recommendations.notes: Combination therapy with third-generation oral cephalosporin plus clindamycin may be used as second-line therapy

recommendations.recommendation: Either doxycycline or a respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin or moxifloxacin) is recommended as alternative agent for empiric antimicrobial therapy in adults who are allergic to penicillin
recommendations.notes: Doxycycline not suitable for children

recommendations.recommendation: Levofloxacin is recommended for children with a history of type I hypersensitivity to penicillin
recommendations.notes: Combination therapy with clindamycin plus third-generation oral cephalosporin for non-type I hypersensitivity

recommendations.recommendation: Routine antimicrobial coverage for S. aureus or MRSA during initial empiric therapy of ABRS is not recommended
recommendations.notes: Based on low recovery rates and potential contamination from nasal flora

recommendations.recommendation: Duration of therapy for uncomplicated ABRS in adults is 5-7 days
recommendations.notes: Shorter courses may improve compliance and reduce adverse events

recommendations.recommendation: In children with ABRS, longer treatment duration of 10-14 days is still recommended
recommendations.notes: Efficacy of shorter courses not specifically studied in pediatric patients

recommendations.recommendation: Intranasal saline irrigations are recommended as adjunctive treatment in adults with ABRS
recommendations.notes: Modest symptom improvement with minimal adverse effects

recommendations.recommendation: Intranasal corticosteroids are recommended as adjunct to antibiotics, primarily in patients with allergic rhinitis history
recommendations.notes: Marginal benefit with NNT of 15, optional recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: Intranasal corticosteroids may be used as adjunctive therapy
recommendations.notes: Mometasone 400mcg/day superior to 200mcg/day

recommendations.recommendation: Neither topical nor oral decongestants and/or antihistamines are recommended as adjunctive treatment
recommendations.notes: May cause rebound congestion and other adverse effects

recommendations.recommendation: Consider alternative management strategy if no improvement after 3-5 days or worsening after 48-72 hours
recommendations.notes: Clinical judgment and monitoring are critical

recommendations.recommendation: Obtain cultures by direct sinus aspiration rather than nasopharyngeal swabs in treatment failures
recommendations.notes: Nasopharyngeal cultures are unreliable

recommendations.recommendation: Use contrast-enhanced CT rather than MRI for suspected suppurative complications
recommendations.notes: CT preferred for availability and speed

recommendations.recommendation: Patients who are seriously ill, immunocompromised, continue to deteriorate clinically despite extended courses of antimicrobial therapy, or have recurrent bouts of acute rhinosinusitis with clearing between episodes should be referred to a specialist
recommendations.notes: Choice of specialist should be based on indication for referral - otolaryngologist, infectious disease specialist, or allergist

recommendations.recommendation: First-line antibiotic therapy with amoxicillin-clavulanate
recommendations.notes: Preferred over amoxicillin alone due to beta-lactamase producing organisms

recommendations.recommendation: Fluoroquinolones (moxifloxacin, levofloxacin) for penicillin-allergic patients
recommendations.notes: Alternative for beta-lactam allergic patients

recommendations.recommendation: Short-course therapy (3-6 days) may be as effective as longer courses
recommendations.notes: Based on meta-analysis of randomized trials

recommendations.recommendation: Intranasal corticosteroids as adjunctive therapy
recommendations.notes: May provide symptom relief when used with antibiotics

recommendations.recommendation: Use endoscopically directed middle meatal cultures versus maxillary sinus taps for bacterial diagnosis
recommendations.notes: Reference to meta-analysis comparing diagnostic methods

recommendations.recommendation: Consider CT and MRI for patients with sinusitis complications
recommendations.notes: Role of imaging in complicated sinusitis cases

medications.dose: Standard dose or high-dose (2g BID adults, 90mg/kg/day BID children)

medications.indication: Alternative to amoxicillin-clavulanate in adults, penicillin allergy
Penicillin allergy in adults and children with type I hypersensitivity

medications.dose: 2g orally twice daily (adults) or 90 mg/kg/day orally twice daily (children)

medications.indication: Single dose IV/IM for children with vomiting
Combination therapy for non-type I penicillin allergy

medications.dose: Children: 200-400mg/kg/day IV every 6h; Adults: 1.5-3g IV every 6h

red_flags: Clinical worsening after 48-72 hours of appropriate therapy
Failure to improve after 3-5 days of appropriate therapy
Suppurative complications such as orbital extension
Failure to respond to both first-line and second-line antimicrobial agents
Clinical worsening despite 72 hours of appropriate therapy
High fever ≥39°C (102°F) with purulent nasal discharge
Severe facial pain lasting 3-4 consecutive days
Severe illness with poor response to initial therapy
High-level macrolide resistance mediated by ermB
Type I hypersensitivity reaction to penicillin
Geographic regions with high endemic rates of PNS S. pneumoniae (≥10%)
Clinical manifestations suggestive of orbital extension of infection
Clinical manifestations suggestive of intracranial extension of infection
Nosocomial sinusitis with prolonged nasal intubation
Signs suggestive of orbital or intracranial suppurative complications
Orbital complications (periorbital edema, diplopia, decreased visual acuity)
Intracranial complications (severe headache, altered mental status)
Severe unilateral facial pain with neurologic deficits
extraction.follow_up: Monitor clinical response at 48-72 hours; reassess if no improvement by 3-5 days; consider imaging for suspected complications; saline irrigation and intranasal corticosteroids as adjunctive therapy
extraction.escalation_path: Specialist referral (otolaryngologist, infectious disease specialist, allergist) for seriously ill/immunocompromised patients, those failing extended antimicrobial courses, recurrent episodes, or suspected complications requiring surgical intervention or advanced imaging
